title: Running a rabbit colony through winter in a hoop house
subtitle: Keeping an outdoor colony healthy and productive in a polytunnel or hoop shelter.
excerpt: An outdoor rabbit colony that works well in summer can fall apart in winter if the wet and cold aren't managed. A simple hoop house or polytunnel over the colony area keeps the ground dry, reduces wind chill, and maintains a stable-enough temperature for does to kindle reliably through the cooler months.
sourceNotes: Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, rabbit chapter. University of Wisconsin Extension, Rabbit Production guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A method of keeping rabbits in a communal pen rather than individual cages, typically on deep litter bedding with breeding does and growing kits together.
A low-cost tunnel structure of bent steel hoops covered with polythene sheet, used as a lightweight shelter for animals or crops.

content.text: in winter needs a dry floor and protection from wind more than it needs warmth. Rabbits are cold-tolerant animals; they handle subfreezing temperatures well in dry conditions but rapidly develop respiratory disease in damp, draughty housing. A
gives the colony weather cover without sealing them into a warm, humid space.
Erect the hoop structure over the existing colony area before the first heavy rain. A 3 m × 6 m tunnel is enough for a colony of fifteen to twenty rabbits.

Leave the ends of the tunnel at least partially open, one end fully open for ventilation, one end with a mesh panel to prevent escape. Do not seal the tunnel entirely; condensation builds up quickly in an enclosed polythene space.
Lay a deep litter base inside: 20,30 cm of wood shavings topped with straw. This insulates from the ground and composts gently to generate low-level heat.

Provide one nest box per doe in the colony, placed in the quietest, least-trafficked corner of the hoop house. Fill each box with hay before a doe is due to kindle, does pull fur to line the nest but appreciate extra insulation in winter. Check nest boxes daily from day 28 of the doe's pregnancy.

Increase hay provision in winter, rabbits eat more fibre when ambient temperatures drop. Keep water drinkers frost-free; rubber bowls are more frost-resistant than ceramic or metal. A doe with kits needs unlimited water access, milk production fails rapidly in a dehydrated doe.

Deep litter in a well-ventilated hoop house can be left for six to eight weeks before a full change in winter. Top up with fresh straw weekly. A full muck-out when the litter is exhausted, done while the rabbits are temporarily penned elsewhere, restores the dry base. Keep does with kits undisturbed until kits are at least three weeks old.
